turns.content: I'm building a side project in Rust and I think I can just call cors::CorsLayer with allowed origins and it will handle everything including preflight requests automatically.

That assumption is close but slightly off. When you configure tower_http::cors::CorsLayer, the layer itself doesn't magically intercept all cross-origin traffic; it must be attached to your router's service layer so that every request passes through the middleware stack before reaching your handlers.
So I just need to add the layer once and it will stop CORS errors everywhere on my server?

Not exactly. Think of tower_http::CorsLayer like a security guard at a door who only lets people in if they have a pass; if you don't configure the guard to check for credentials, then requests with Authorization headers will be blocked even though the origin is allowed. You need to set allow_credentials(true) and match your specific allowed origins list carefully.
Hey, I'm getting a preflight error in production and I think I just need to add the origin header to my request body using Json<serde_json::Value> like a normal API call.

That won't work because CORS is handled by a middleware layer before your handler runs, so you can't just set headers in the request payload. You need to configure tower_http::cors::CorsLayer with allowed origins and set the credentials flag if your frontend sends cookies or authorization data.
Okay so where exactly do I call that configuration on my axum service builder? Is it a method on the ServiceExt trait?

It's not part of ServiceExt, you apply it by calling .layer(cors_layer) on your router. Think of it like installing a security gate between the client and your server; if the client asks for permission (preflight), that gate checks if the origin is trusted and whether credentials should be allowed before letting any real data through.
